title: Add HTMX polling jitter to prevent thundering herd
analysis: All 5 dashboard sections in index.html poll at fixed intervals (10s, 10s, 15s, 30s, 60s) via hx-trigger='load, every Xs'. Multiple browser tabs or instances will synchronize their polls, creating a thundering herd effect on the backend. HTMX has no native jitter support, so the fix uses a JS-based approach: replace fixed 'every Xs' triggers with a custom 'poll' event, add 'data-poll-base' attributes declaring each section's base interval, and add a small script in base.html that listens to htmx:afterSettle and schedules the next poll with ±20% random jitter via setTimeout + htmx.trigger(). This is event-driven (not setInterval), so jitter accumulates naturally across cycles, preventing re-synchronization. Existing tests (TestMorphSwap, TestHtmxTimeout, TestAriaLivePollingsections, TestIndex) don't reference 'every Xs' patterns and will continue to pass unchanged.

steps.description: Write TestPollingJitter class in test_app.py: assert each of the 5 sections (orchestrators, performance, memory, memory-graphs, recon) has a data-poll-base attribute with the correct interval (10, 30, 10, 60, 15), and hx-trigger contains 'poll' instead of 'every'
Update index.html: change all 5 sections from hx-trigger='load, every Xs' to hx-trigger='load, poll' and add data-poll-base='X' attribute to each section element

Write TestPollingJitterScript class in test_app.py: assert the rendered HTML contains the jitter script with key markers — htmx:afterSettle listener, data-poll-base reference, htmx.trigger call, setTimeout usage, and Math.random for randomization

Add jitter polling script to base.html (alongside existing error handler): IIFE that listens to htmx:afterSettle, reads data-poll-base from the settled element, computes a jittered delay (base ± 20%), and uses setTimeout + htmx.trigger(el, 'poll') to schedule the next fetch

Write test_no_fixed_polling_intervals in TestPollingJitter: assert the rendered index page HTML contains zero occurrences of 'every ' followed by digits and 's' in any hx-trigger attribute — anti-regression guard against reintroducing fixed polling

Verify all tests pass (step-5 test should already pass given step-2 implementation). Run full dashboard test suite to confirm no regressions in existing TestMorphSwap, TestHtmxTimeout, TestAriaLivePollingsections, or TestIndex tests

design_decisions.decision: Use event-driven polling (htmx:afterSettle → setTimeout → htmx.trigger) instead of setInterval with jitter
design_decisions.rationale: Event-driven approach naturally prevents timer overlap — the next poll is only scheduled after the current response settles. setInterval would fire regardless of whether the previous request completed, and could pile up requests during slow responses.

20% provides enough randomization to desynchronize multiple tabs (e.g., a 10s base yields 8-12s actual intervals) without making the polling feel unpredictable to users. This is a standard jitter ratio used in distributed systems.

design_decisions.decision: Use data-poll-base custom attribute instead of embedding intervals in JS
design_decisions.rationale: Declarative approach keeps interval configuration in the template alongside other HTMX attributes. Adding a new polling section only requires HTML attributes, no JS changes. Also easily testable via HTML assertions.

design_decisions.decision: Place jitter script in base.html, not index.html
design_decisions.rationale: Follows existing pattern — the HTMX error handler script is already in base.html. The jitter script is generic and would apply to any page with data-poll-base sections. Keeps index.html clean (template structure only).

design_decisions.decision: Use custom 'poll' event in hx-trigger instead of re-implementing hx-trigger programmatically
design_decisions.rationale: hx-trigger='load, poll' is clean HTMX — 'load' handles initial fetch, 'poll' is triggered by our JS. No need to manipulate HTMX internals or use hx-trigger attributes dynamically.

reuse.how: All new tests use the client fixture to GET '/' and inspect rendered HTML, same pattern as TestMorphSwap, TestHtmxTimeout, etc.
Use html.index('hx-get="/partials/..."') to locate a section, then assert on attributes within a slice — same technique for checking data-poll-base values per section.

reuse.what: IIFE pattern from existing error handler script
reuse.how: Wrap jitter script in an IIFE to avoid global scope pollution, add event listener to document.body, same pattern as error handler.
